title: Quick check: what Managed SIEM is

questions.prompt: Course 7 is described as optional. What does "optional" mean here?
choices: The course only applies if your MSP subscribes to Huntress Managed SIEM; if not, skip Course 7 and pick up at Course 8.

choices.label: The course is recommended but not graded.
choices.rationale: Optional refers to product subscription, not assessment weight.

choices.label: SIEM doesn't differ from EDR or ITDR, so you can skip it.
choices.rationale: The course exists because SIEM adds new sources and diagnostics. The optional flag is about whether the MSP buys the product.

choices.label: The course is being retired in a future release.
choices.rationale: Nothing in the lesson signals retirement.

questions.explanation: Optional means the MSP may or may not subscribe to Managed SIEM. If not, Course 7 doesn't apply day-to-day.

questions.prompt: When a SIEM Incident Report lands, what about the response shape is the same as EDR and ITDR?

choices.label: The tech writes detections after reviewing the incident.
choices.rationale: Detection writing is above the SIEM ceiling.

choices.label: The tech investigates the log lines from scratch before acting.
choices.rationale: The SOC has already triaged. Investigating from scratch is the cardinal mistake.

choices: The header structure, severity bands, SOC-has-triaged keystone, and read-the-Recommendation-and-act discipline.

choices.label: The customer's IT contact triages the report before it reaches the helpdesk.
choices.rationale: The Incident Report comes from the SOC, not the customer.

questions.explanation: The skeleton, severity bands, reading order, and Recommendation discipline are identical across all three surfaces.

questions.prompt: Which of these is something SIEM sees that EDR and ITDR don't?

choices.label: Process trees on Windows endpoints with the Huntress agent.
choices.rationale: That is EDR territory.

choices: Pre-endpoint activity: firewall, VPN, or SaaS log activity from before an attacker has a foot on an endpoint.

choices.label: OAuth grants on Microsoft 365 mailboxes.
choices.rationale: That is ITDR's surface.

choices.label: Whether AV is installed on a host.
choices.rationale: Not a visibility question on any Huntress surface.

questions.explanation: SIEM ingests logs from sources EDR and ITDR don't reach, catching pre-endpoint movement in firewall, VPN, and SaaS logs.

questions.prompt: A new tech says "SIEM means I have to learn to write detections." What is the correct response?

choices.label: Detection writing is optional but encouraged once the tech is confident.
choices.rationale: Detection writing is not a tech action regardless of confidence.

choices.label: Only senior techs write detections; helpdesk runs them in a sandbox first.
choices.rationale: This invents a workflow the lesson doesn't describe. Detection writing belongs to Huntress.

choices.label: Detections are auto-generated from the customer's RMM data.
choices.rationale: The Huntress SOC writes and tunes detections, not the RMM.

choices: Managed SIEM means Huntress owns detection writing and tuning. The tech responds to Incident Reports. Detection authorship is above the ceiling.
questions.explanation: Managed SIEM means Huntress writes and tunes detections. The helpdesk responds to Incident Reports the same way as on EDR and ITDR.

questions.prompt: A peer claims SIEM is an alternative to EDR and ITDR and you can pick one. Why is that wrong?

choices.label: SIEM costs more, so customers stack them for budget reasons.
choices.rationale: Wrong commercial framing. The reason is complementary visibility, not price.

choices: The three surfaces are complementary. Customers with all three see across endpoints, identities, and log sources together.

choices.label: EDR and ITDR are being deprecated in favour of SIEM.
choices.rationale: All three remain active surfaces.

choices.label: Huntress only sells the three together as a bundle.
choices.rationale: SIEM is optional per subscription.

questions.explanation: EDR, ITDR, and SIEM see different things. They complement each other rather than compete.

questions.prompt: How is Managed SIEM priced compared to traditional SIEMs?

choices.label: Per-event-volume with hourly billing.
choices.rationale: That is what Huntress differentiates against, not what they do.

choices.label: Per-endpoint, same as EDR.
choices.rationale: SIEM is per-source, not per-endpoint.

choices: Per-data-source with predictable pricing, in contrast to event-volume pricing common elsewhere.

choices.label: Free for the first 90 days, then per-endpoint.
choices.rationale: Invented detail not in the lesson.

questions.explanation: Huntress prices Managed SIEM per data source with predictable costs, rather than per event volume.

questions.prompt: How does the Evidence in a SIEM Incident Report typically look different from an EDR report?
choices: Log lines with timestamps, source IPs, and event types rather than process trees or sign-in details.

choices.label: Identical to EDR: process trees and command-line strings.
choices.rationale: Process trees are EDR's Evidence shape.

choices.label: A free-text summary from the customer's IT contact.
choices.rationale: Evidence comes from log data, not the customer.

choices.label: Screenshots of the SOC analyst's workstation.
choices.rationale: Invented and unrelated to the platform.

questions.explanation: SIEM Evidence contains log lines with timestamps and fields from one or more sources, not the process trees of EDR or sign-in events of ITDR.

questions.prompt: You blocked a firewall destination per a SIEM Recommendation. EDR shows no related incident on the named host. The Recommendation said "check for EDR signals," not "isolate." What do you do?

choices.label: Conclude the firewall log was a false positive since EDR doesn't agree.
choices.rationale: SIEM's value is catching pre-endpoint signal before EDR sees it. Absence of EDR signal does not override the SIEM Recommendation.

choices.label: Open an EDR isolation on the host because SIEM raised an incident on it.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation said "check," not "isolate." Adding isolation goes beyond what the SOC asked for.

choices: Document the EDR clean state in the Incident Report note. Close with the firewall block complete. The cross-check is documentation, not a veto.
questions.explanation: The Recommendation asked for an EDR cross-check. You did it, the result was clean, and that finding goes into documentation. Absence of EDR signal does not override the SIEM Recommendation.

questions.prompt: Match Managed SIEM to what it primarily sees.

choices.label: Endpoint telemetry: process trees and persistence mechanisms.
choices.rationale: That is EDR's primary visibility.

choices.label: Identity events: sign-ins, mailbox rules, OAuth grants.
choices.rationale: That is ITDR's primary visibility.

choices: Logs across sources: network device logs, firewall logs, application logs, SaaS logs EDR and ITDR don't cover.

choices.label: Patch compliance percentages and vendor release notes.
choices.rationale: Patch compliance is the RMM's job, not any Huntress surface.

questions.explanation: SIEM ingests logs across sources the other surfaces don't reach: firewalls, VPN, SaaS apps, custom applications.

questions.prompt: A High SIEM Incident Report lands. The Recommendation is to block a firewall destination and cross-check EDR. A tech says: "SIEM is unfamiliar, I should escalate to a senior." Is that the right call?

choices.label: Yes. Any SIEM-tagged report is senior territory until formal training.
choices.rationale: The disposition carries across surfaces. Unfamiliarity with SIEM-shaped Evidence is not an escalation trigger.

choices: No. Read the Recommendation in the standard order, run the firewall block per the runbook, cross-check EDR. The fundamentals carry across.

choices.label: Yes. Block the firewall destination first, then escalate before the EDR cross-check.
choices.rationale: The EDR cross-check is in scope and the escalation is not warranted.

questions.explanation: The reading order, Recommendation discipline, and cross-surface verification carry across from EDR and ITDR. Unfamiliarity is not itself an escalation trigger.
